metadata.description: HealthFit AI Structured Knowledge Base — Contains categorised health and fitness reference data that can be expanded independently of the intent dataset.
metadata.disclaimer: This information is for general wellness purposes only. It does not constitute medical advice. Always consult a qualified healthcare professional for personalised guidance.

categories.label: Underweight
categories.advice: You may need to increase calorie intake and consult a doctor if significantly underweight.

categories.label: Normal Weight
categories.advice: Excellent! Maintain your current lifestyle with balanced nutrition and regular exercise.

categories.label: Overweight
categories.advice: Consider moderate dietary changes and increase physical activity. Aim for 150 minutes of exercise per week.

categories.label: Obese
categories.advice: It is recommended to consult a doctor or dietitian for a structured weight management plan.

calorie_guidelines.bmr_note: Basal Metabolic Rate (BMR) is the number of calories your body needs at complete rest to maintain basic functions.

activity_multipliers.level: Extra active (very hard exercise + physical job)

calorie_guidelines.weight_loss_deficit: A daily calorie deficit of 300–500 kcal leads to a healthy weight loss of 0.3–0.5 kg per week.
calorie_guidelines.weight_gain_surplus: A daily calorie surplus of 250–400 kcal supports lean muscle gain when combined with strength training.

tips: Start every morning with a glass of water before any food.
Carry a reusable water bottle and sip regularly throughout the day.
Eat water-rich foods such as cucumber, watermelon, oranges, and lettuce.
Set hourly reminders to drink water if you tend to forget.
Increase intake during hot weather, illness, or intense physical activity.

sleep_hygiene_tips: Maintain a consistent sleep and wake time, even on weekends.
Avoid screens (phone, TV, laptop) for at least 1 hour before bedtime.
Keep your bedroom cool, dark, and free from noise.
Avoid caffeine after 3 PM and alcohol close to bedtime.
Engage in a relaxing bedtime routine: reading, gentle stretching, or meditation.
Avoid heavy meals within 2 hours of sleep.
Regular exercise improves sleep quality — but avoid intense training within 3 hours of bed.

fats_to_limit: Saturated fats in excess (fried foods, processed meats)

vitamins_and_minerals.sources: Oranges, guava, bell peppers, kiwi, lemon
Sunlight exposure, fatty fish, egg yolk, fortified milk
Spinach, lentils, red meat, tofu, pumpkin seeds

vitamins_and_minerals.benefits: Bone and teeth strength, muscle function
vitamins_and_minerals.sources: Dairy, ragi, sesame seeds, leafy greens, tofu
Nuts, seeds, dark chocolate, leafy greens, bananas

vitamins_and_minerals.benefits: Heart health, muscle contractions, blood pressure
vitamins_and_minerals.sources: Banana, sweet potato, coconut water, lentils, avocado
Meat, shellfish, pumpkin seeds, chickpeas, nuts

workout_principles.progressive_overload: Gradually increase weight, reps, or sets over time to continuously challenge muscles and drive adaptation.
workout_principles.warm_up: Always begin with 5–10 minutes of light cardio and dynamic stretches to raise core body temperature and reduce injury risk.
workout_principles.cool_down: End every session with 5–10 minutes of static stretching and slow breathing to aid recovery and flexibility.

exercise_mental_health_benefits: Reduces symptoms of depression and anxiety
mindfulness_tips: Practice 10 minutes of meditation daily using apps like Headspace or Insight Timer.
Engage in deep breathing exercises — inhale for 4 counts, hold for 4, exhale for 6.
Spend time in nature — even a 15-minute walk in a park reduces cortisol significantly.
Practice gratitude journalling — write 3 things you are grateful for each morning.
Limit news and social media consumption if it triggers anxiety.

common_fitness_myths.fact: Women have lower testosterone levels than men, making significant bulk extremely unlikely without specific supplementation and training.

common_fitness_myths.myth: You must exercise every day to see results.
common_fitness_myths.fact: Rest days are when muscles repair and grow. Overtraining can lead to injury and burnout. 3–5 days per week is optimal for most people.

common_fitness_myths.myth: Spot reduction of fat is possible.
common_fitness_myths.fact: You cannot choose where your body loses fat. Overall fat loss through a calorie deficit reduces fat throughout the body gradually.
Excess calories — from any macronutrient — cause weight gain. Carbohydrates are the body's primary energy source and are essential for performance.

common_fitness_myths.myth: More sweat means a better workout.
common_fitness_myths.fact: Sweat is simply the body's cooling mechanism. It does not directly correlate with calorie burn or workout effectiveness.

emergency_resources.disclaimer: This chatbot is NOT a substitute for emergency medical services. In case of any medical emergency, contact emergency services immediately.
